segments.s386-g9: چنین گفت is a frozen narrative formula meaning “thus spoke/said.” کامد is an archaic third‑person participial/finite verb meaning “coming/arriving” and here qualifies سوار (the rider). ز is the poetic preposition “from,” so the noun phrase literally reads “the rider coming from Turan.” Word order is adjective/participle (کامد) before the head (سوار) as often in classical Persian.
segments.s386-g10: بپویم and بگویم are first‑person present/future forms with the prefix بـ (representing the present/future narrative), so the speaker says “I will go” (بپویم) and “I will say/tell” (بگویم). به marks the indirect object/goal (“to Esfandiyar”). The two verbs are coordinated: “I will go (there) and tell Esfandiyar.”
segments.s386-g11: فرود آمد is a past tense verbal phrase meaning “he alighted / dismounted.” The following string از باره دژ دوان is poetic, with دوان (a verbal adjective ‘galloping’ or ‘having run’) modifying the scene; together it conveys that he had ridden up to or galloped to the fortress. In classical word order modifiers and verbal adjectives can follow the nouns they relate to, producing this compressed idiom.
segments.s386-g12: چنین گفت again = “thus said.” کای is the vocative particle “O” (addressing someone). نامور پهلوان is a nominal epithet, literally “renowned champion.” The clause is the speaker addressing someone directly after alighting.
segments.s386-g13: سواری همی بینم is a present/habitual narrative clause meaning “I see a rider” (همی gives the continuous or customary sense in classical Persian narrative). از دیدگاه literally “from (my) view” = “in sight / within view.” The phrase sets up what is visible to the speaker.
segments.s386-g14: کلاهی به سر بر نهاده سیاه is a descriptive participial clause: کلاهی (a hat) + به سر (on the head) + بر نهاده (placed upon; a perfective/verbal adjective) + سیاه (black) — literally “a hat, black, placed on (his) head.” The verb phrase بر نهاده is a past participle used adjectivally to describe the rider.
segments.s386-g15: شوم is an exclamatory/optative word here (literally “may I become [ill‑fated]”) used as an interjection expressing dread or ill omen: “Alas!” باز بینم = “again I see.” که introduces the content clause “that …” and گشتاسپیست is a nominal predicate meaning “he is of Goshtasp” (i.e., belonging to Goshtasp’s side). The overall sense: the speaker fears that the rider belongs to Goshtasp.
segments.s386-g16: وگر here means “but if / or else.” کینه‌جویست is a predicate meaning “he is vengeful / vindictive.” و is the coordinating conjunction “and.” ارجاسپیست is another nominal predicate meaning “he is of Arjasp” (i.e., on Arjasp’s side). The clause offers an alternative: if not Goshtasp’s man, then a vindictive fellow aligned with Arjasp.
segments.s386-g145: چو = temporal conjunction 'when'. آمد به هوش = a fixed idiom 'came to (his) senses/awoke' with آمد (past of آمدن) + به + هوش (awareness). آن = 'that', pointing back to a previously mentioned person. گو here is an archaic attributive element attached to the noun phrase (used in Shahnameh to mark a spoken/named or emphatic epithet), and زورمند = adjective 'powerful, mighty' modifying that person.
segments.s386-g146: همی is an archaic/clitic particle often used in epic poetry to mark or emphasize the action (here giving a promptive sense like 'forthwith' or continuous past). پیش بنهاد is a compound meaning 'placed/put before' (پیش 'before' + بنهاد, past of نهادن 'to place/put'); زنجیر و بند = 'chains and fetters' (two coordinated nouns). The verb is distributed over the coordinated objects.
segments.s386-g147: چنین گفت = 'thus he said' (چنین 'thus' + گفت 'said'); کاین is a contracted form of که این 'that these/that which', introducing the quoted content. هدیه‌های = plural 'gifts/offerings'. گرزم here is a genitival or attributive form 'of my mace' or 'mace-related' (گرز = mace), i.e. 'the gifts of the mace' or 'these mace-gifts'.
segments.s386-g148: منش پست بادش is an optative/wish clause: منش پست = 'low/base character'; بادش is the old optative/existential form ('may it be to him' / 'may he have') with the enclitic ش (his). به بزم و به رزم = 'in feast and in battle' (two adverbial phrases joined by و). The whole line is a malediction or wish against the recipient of the gifts.
segments.s386-g149: به گرمابه شد = 'went to/entered the public bath' where به + noun + شد is a common periphrasis for motion/arrival ('went to the bath'); با تن دردمند = 'with a pained/aching body' (با = with, تن = body, دردمند = suffering/in pain) describing his condition as he entered.
segments.s386-g150: ز زنجیر = 'because of/from the chain' (ز = 'from' in poetic register); فرسوده و مستمند are predicates/adjectives describing his state as caused by the chain: فرسوده 'worn-out, exhausted' and مستمند here 'afflicted/poor/needy' (i.e. weakened, suffering). Grammatically these adjectives link back to the subject implied in the previous segment.
segments.s386-g151: چو آمد = 'when he came'. به در = 'to the door/threshold' (به + در where در = gate/door). پس here functions as 'after/behind' (indicating position or sequence: 'after/behind him'). گو is the same archaic attributive element seen above (marking the following epithet), and نامدار = 'renowned, famed' modifies the person who arrives.
segments.s386-g152: رخش بود = 'his Rakhsh (the steed) was' (رخش = the famed steed's name/common noun 'steed'); همچون گل اندر بهار = simile 'like a flower in spring' (همچون = like, گل = flower, اندر = in/within, بهار = spring). The verb بود is simple past 'was' linking subject and simile.
segments.s386-g17: اگر introduces a conditional clause (“if”). ترک is the predicate noun of the condition (“[he] is a Turk”). باشد is the classical/polite subjunctive/present-form of ‘to be’ used here to mark the condition. ببرم is a first‑person verb form (subjunctive/intentive in older Persian) meaning “I will take/carry,” and سرش is the direct object (‘his head’) with the enclitic possessive -ش attached to سر.
segments.s386-g18: به خاک is a prepositional phrase ‘to/into the earth/ground’ placed before the verb. افگنم is an archaic 1st‑person verb form from افکندن (here meaning ‘I cast/throw’) used like an intentive/future; it takes the previously mentioned head as its object. نابسوده is a negative adjective/adverbial (نا‑ + بسوده) qualifying the result — ‘not ...’ — and together with برش (here referring back to the head/object) completes the clause (“not leaving it untouched/unchastened”).
segments.s386-g19: چنین گفت is a fixed phrase ‘thus said / thus spoke.’ پرمایه is an epithet placed before the name (an adjective meaning ‘substantial / mighty’), and اسفندیار is the proper name of the speaker. Word order is adjective + name following the verb phrase.
segments.s386-g20: که introduces the reported clause (‘that/which’). راه گذر is a compound meaning ‘the road / pass’ (راه + گذر). کی is the interrogative ‘who’ (classical usage, here functioning like ‘who ever’). بوده is the perfect/past participial form ‘has been / was’ used here with an interrogative to form ‘who has been/ever been,’ and بی‌سوار (بی‑ + سوار) is an adverbial adjective ‘unmounted/without a horse’ describing the manner of passing.
segments.s386-g21: همانا is an exclamatory particle ‘verily / behold.’ کز is the literary form of از meaning ‘from,’ so کز ایران = ‘from Iran.’ یکی here is an indefinite article-like word ‘a certain / one’ placed before لشکری to mean ‘a certain troop,’ and لشکری denotes a military force or troop.
segments.s386-g22: سوی ما بیامد is the verb phrase ‘came toward us’ (سوی = toward, ما = us, بیامد = came). به پیغمبری is a prepositional phrase indicating manner or accompaniment — ‘with/as a prophet / in the guise of a prophet’ — where به introduces the role or manner (classical usage).
segments.s386-g23: کلاهی ... نهاده is a verb‑noun construction: کلاهی (a hat) + به سر (on the head) + بر نهاده (has put/placed on) — literally ‘having placed a hat on (his) head.’ بر is the preposition ‘on/upon,’ نهاده is the past participle ‘placed/put,’ and دوپر is a postpositive descriptor ‘two‑plumed / double‑feathered’ modifying the hat.
segments.s386-g24: ز بیم is a causal phrase ‘from/because of fear’ (ز = from/because of in poetic register, بیم = fear). سواران is the plural ‘the horsemen/riders’ and پرخاشخر is an adjectival/epithet meaning ‘quarrelsome/aggressive,’ so the whole phrase says he was made quarrelsome by fear of the riders (the cause phrase precedes the affected adjective).
segments.s386-g73: This clause opens with the verb براندیش (an older/poetic infinitive or imperative form meaning 'consider' or 'take into account'), followed by کان, an archaic linking/relativizing particle introducing the content of that consideration. پیر لهراسپ را is a noun phrase with پیر (venerable/old) modifying the name لهراسپ, and را is the definite/object marker marking Lohrasp as the specific person being spoken of.
segments.s386-g74: پرستنده is a noun (participle-like) meaning 'worshipper' or 'devotee' describing پیر لهراسپ; و simply links the next phrase; باب here functions as a figurative term 'source/progenitor' (older usage), followed by the proper name گشتاسپ and the object marker را. The clause omits an explicit copula ('was') as is normal in Persian narrative style.
segments.s386-g75: پسر به که is an interrogative/indefinite opening meaning 'which son' or 'what son'; جوید is a present/poetic-form verb 'seeks' (here rendered as 'would seek' in English), with همی as an archaic continuous/aspectual particle that accompanies the verb to indicate ongoing or habitual action. کین اوی is 'his/their vengeance' or 'vengeance upon him' with اوی an old third‑person pronoun referring back to the person mentioned earlier.
segments.s386-g76: This relative clause (که ...) describes that person: تخت پدر داشت is a simple past construction 'held/had his father's throne' (داشت here = 'had/held'), و links to ایین اوی where ایین means 'rite/ceremony' or 'custom' and اوی is the possessive/third‑person pronoun 'his', so 'and his rites'. Word order is typical Persian: relative pronoun (که) + verb phrase.
segments.s386-g77: بدو گفت is 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him'; گفت here is the past-tense verb 'said' in narrative; the provided dictionary glosses are conversational senses but in context گفت functions as speech-past). ار introduces a conditional 'if', and ایدونک is an emphatic/temporal particle ('now' / 'at this point' / 'if at present') often found in older Persian. کین نیا = 'the vengeance of the ancestor' with نیا as 'ancestor'.
segments.s386-g78: نجویی is a noun/masdar meaning 'a search/quest' or 'pursuit' and نداری literally 'you do not have' (here 'you lack'); به دل is the locative phrase 'in the heart'; کیمیا is 'alchemy' or in poetic usage 'the elixir/secret of transmutation/immortality.' Together the clause says you lack any pursuit of the elixir in your heart. The verb 'نداری' is second person present/imperative-negated form 'you do not have'.
segments.s386-g79: همای خردمند is a nominal phrase: همای (the mythical bird Homay/Homa) modified by خردمند 'wise'. و به آفرید is a compact verbal phrase 'and he created/made' (و links, به here is the stylistic preverb/particle often found before verbs in classical Persian, and آفرید is the past 'created').
segments.s386-g80: که introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that/that'; باد هوا is a compound image 'wind of desire' (باد = wind, هوا = desire/whim), روی ایشان = 'their faces' (روی = face; ایشان = they/them polite/archaic), and ندید is past 'did not see' (negated seeing). The clause thus says the winds of caprice did not 'see' (i.e., affect) them.
segments.s386-g105: This is a conditional front clause: 'اگر' introduces the condition ('if'). Word order is subject 'من' (I) + time adverb 'کنون' (now) followed by the object/feeling phrase 'کین بسیچم'. 'کین' = hatred/vengeance and 'بسیچم' is a first‑person, literary/poetic expression indicating having or nursing it intensively (the -م is the 1st‑person ending). The clause ends with the interrogative 'چه سود' = 'what use/benefit'.
segments.s386-g106: 'کزیشان' is the literary contraction of 'کز ایشان' = 'from them/that from them' and links this subordinate idea to the question 'چه سود'. 'برآورد' here is a literary verb meaning 'bring forth/produce' (third person) and 'بدخواه دود' is the agent+object: 'the ill‑wisher's smoke' (i.e. smoke produced by a hostile person). The whole segment explains why nursing hatred would be useless.
segments.s386-g107: 'چو' here = 'when' (temporal). The proper name 'جاماسپ' is the subject. 'زین گونه' = 'of this sort / such' (literary phrasing) modifies 'پاسخ'. 'شنود' is the classical 3rd‑person verb 'he heard' (literary form). The clause is a temporal clause: 'When Jamasp heard such an answer...'.
segments.s386-g108: Structure: possessor+verb+complement. 'دلش' = 'his heart' (دل + enclitic ش = his). 'گشت' = 'became' (literary past/ resultative). 'از درد' = 'from/through sorrow' (cause), and 'پر داغ و دود' = 'full of burning grief and smoke' where 'پر' is the adjective 'full (of)'. The phrase is a poetic metaphor: heart filled with grief (داغ) and smoke (دود).
segments.s386-g109: 'همی بود' is a habitual/literary past construction ('he was/stood'). 'بر پای' means 'on his feet' (i.e. standing). The conjunction 'و' links the next phrase 'دل پر ز خشم' = 'a heart full of anger' ('پر' = full of, 'ز' = from/of). The segment describes his posture and inner state.
segments.s386-g110: Adverbial phrase 'به زاری' = 'with lamentation / lamenting' (here 'به' functions as an instrumental/‑ly), followed by the verb 'همی راند' (literary past: 'he drove/expelled/forced'). 'آب از دو چشم' = 'water from both eyes' — a conventional poetic description of shedding tears. Word order puts the manner first, then the verbal action and the object.
segments.s386-g111: 'بدو گفت' = 'he said to him' (literally 'to him said'); 'بدو' is the dative 'to him'. 'گفت' here is the verb 'said' (the provided lexical senses omitted the verbal reading, so note that this is the speech verb). 'کای' is the vocative 'O' addressing 'پهلوان جهان' = 'champion of the world' (an honorific).
segments.s386-g112: This is a prototypical conditional clause: 'اگر' = 'if', 'تیره' = 'dark/ominous', 'گردد' = 'becomes' (literary present/subjunctive/optative form), 'دلت' = 'your heart' (دل + 2nd‑person enclitic ت), and 'با روان' = 'with (your) spirit/soul' (meaning 'together with your soul' or 'along with your spirit'). The clause is unfinished here (continues in the next line), but grammatically it sets a condition about the state of the addressee's heart and spirit.
segments.s386-g41: درود is a noun 'greeting'; شهنشاه ایران is a genitive phrase 'the Shahanshah of Iran' (noun + place name). دهی is the classical 2nd‑person form of دادن used optatively/imperatively ('give' / 'may you give'), so the clause reads literally 'Give (a) greeting (to) the Shahanshah of Iran.' Word order here places the object noun phrase before the verb for poetic effect.
segments.s386-g42: This clause is a poetic inversion: ز + noun ('from/of knowledge') introduces the cause or source; ندارد is 3rd‑person singular 'does not have'; دلت is دل ('heart') + enclitic -ت 'your' and functions as the subject/possessor; آگهی is the noun 'awareness/information'. Literal order would be 'دلت آگهی ندارد' — 'your heart lacks awareness.'
segments.s386-g43: درودم is درود + possessive suffix -م 'my greeting.' آمد is the past/punctual verb 'came' and کنون 'now' is a temporal adverb; از ارجاسپ specifies the origin ('from Arjasp'). The sequence is a straightforward nominal + verbal clause: 'My greeting came from Arjasp now.'
segments.s386-g44: کز is the classical relative/conjunction 'that/for/which' introducing the consequence clause. همی is the archaic/poetic particle often marking aspect/emphasis (classical continuous/inevitable sense). دست is 'hand(s)' used literally here; شوید is the verb 'become' (from شدن) in an old poetic form meaning 'will be / become' (third person); به خون 'into/with blood' marks the result. Together: 'that from Iran the hands will become stained with blood.'
segments.s386-g45: مرا is the object 'me' split in the segmentation as م + را (م = me, را = definite/object marker). کردند is the past plural 'they did/made' used as a causative/action verb ('they bound'). بند here means 'fetter'/'bond' (object of کردند). بر بی‌گناه = 'against/though innocent' or more naturally 'while (I was) innocent' — بر works as a preposition introducing the circumstance. Overall: 'They fettered/bound me though I was innocent.'
segments.s386-g46: همانا is an emphatic adverb 'verily/truly.' گه here means 'time/occasion'; رزم is 'battle'; فرزند شاه is a genitive 'son (or prince) of the king.' The phrase sets a temporal scene: 'truly, at the time/occasion of the king's son's battle.'
segments.s386-g47: چنین بود is a copular inversion meaning 'such was' or 'thus it was.' پاداش رنج مرا = 'the reward of my suffering' where مرا (here split as م + را) marks 'my' (possessive + object marker). The clause states the result/evaluation: 'Such was the reward of my pain.'
segments.s386-g48: به is the preposition 'to/with/into'; آهن is 'iron' (used poetically for metal/armour or iron fetters); بیاراست is a verbal form meaning 'adorned/arrayed/put on' (perfective/action completed); گنج مرا = 'my treasure' (again م + را). The literal sense: 'he adorned/my treasure with iron' — i.e., he put iron on/bound my treasure (likely iron fetters or iron coverings).
segments.s386-g161: چه کرد is an interrogative/periphrastic phrase 'what did (he) do' with its subject omitted (typical of epic poetry). این چمان باره بربری is a noun phrase: این 'this' + چمان 'such' + باره 'case/instance' (i.e. 'this sort/case') + بربری 'barbarian/barbaric' (noun/adjective); the whole gives 'this barbarian of such a kind.' Word order is demonstrative + modifiers + noun, with the verb preceding the noun phrase for rhetorical effect.
segments.s386-g162: چه بایست کردن is a fixed interrogative asking 'what ought one to do': چه 'what' + بایست 'must/ought' + کردن used as a light/auxiliary verb. بدین لاغری is a prepositional phrase meaning 'with/to this thinness' (بدین = به + این 'to/with this' in fused poetic form) + لاغری 'emaciation, thinness'. Note the periphrastic obligation (بایست + کردن) and that the demonstrative is fused to the preposition.
segments.s386-g163: بشویید is a 2nd-person-plural (or polite/plural imperative/subjunctive) form 'wash (him)'. و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. او را is the object pronoun 'him' with the object marker را making it definite/emphatic. بی‌آهو کنید is 'make (him) بی‌آهو' (بی- here 'without' or an adjectival/resultative formation with آهو 'deer'); کنید is the 2nd-person-plural imperative/subjunctive of کردن 'to make/do', so the clause means 'wash him and make him بی‌آهو' (an idiomatic/resultative description).
segments.s386-g164: به خوردن is a means/agent phrase 'by/through eating' where به + infinitive (خوردن) expresses manner or means. تن ش را is the possessed noun 'his body' (تن 'body' + ش 'his' ) with را as object marker. به نیرو کنید is a result clause 'make (it) into strength / make (him) strong' where به indicates direction/result ('into') and نیرو 'strength' is the result noun; کنید again is the plural imperative/causative 'make.'
segments.s386-g165: فرستاد is a 3rd-person-singular past 'he sent' or 'he dispatched.' کس is an indefinite 'someone/anyone' as direct object. نزد here marks direction/location 'to/at the place of' and functions like 'to the presence of'; آهنگران is the plural noun 'blacksmiths/metalworkers.' The whole clause is a simple past transitive construction 'he sent someone to the blacksmiths.'
segments.s386-g166: هرانکس که ... اندران is a relative/free-choice clause: هرانکس 'whoever, any one who' + که the relative particle 'who/that' + استاد 'master/expert' + بود past copula 'was' + اندران 'among them/therein.' Together: 'whoever among them was a master.' Note the postposition اندران (= اندر + ان) marking inclusion 'among them.'
segments.s386-g167: برفتند is the simple past plural 'they went/departed.' و is 'and.' چندی is an adverb/pronominal 'some, several' (here modifying زره). زره 'armor' is the object. خواستند is past plural 'they requested/wanted' (here 'asked for'). The clause means 'they departed and asked for some armor.'
segments.s386-g168: سلیحش 'his arms/weaponry' is the noun سلیح 'arms' with the possessive clitic -ش 'his'. یکایک is an adverb 'one by one / each in turn.' بپیراستند is past plural of پیراستن with the prefix ب- (بپیراستند) meaning 'they adorned/arrayed/equipped' — here 'they equipped his weapons one by one.' Note the verb's classical form with prefix b- and past plural ending.
segments.s386-g33: بدادش is an archaic/potential compound of the verb 'داد' with a preceding b- (poetic) plus the enclitic pronoun -ش (him), so it functions as a past-tense verb phrase 'gave him' or 'bestowed on him'. درود پدر سربسر is a noun phrase: درود 'greeting' followed by its possessor پدر 'father', and سربسر 'all over / everywhere' is an adverbial intensifier placed after the noun phrase.
segments.s386-g34: پیامی که آورده is a relative clause: پیامی (a message, with the -ی ending here functioning as an indefinite/masculine form) + که 'that/who' introducing the clause + آورده, perfect/past participial form 'has brought / brought'. بد در بدر is a short adjectival/phrastic comment: بد 'bad/ill' modifies the following word بدر (here a proper or poetic name), giving 'bad at/for Badr' or 'ill to Badr'.
segments.s386-g35: چنین (thus) precedes the noun پاسخ (answer/reply). آورد is a past tense verb 'brought/uttered/related' and here takes پاسخ as its object; the name اسفندیار follows the verb (verb–subject order is common in classical Persian), so the clause reads 'Thus Esfandiyar rendered an answer.'
segments.s386-g36: که‌ای here functions as the opening of Esfandiyar's address: که (that/which) plus the vocative ای (O) — effectively a poetic 'O who/you'. از خرد در جهان یادگار is a nominal phrase 'a remnant/monument (یادگار) of wisdom (خرد) in the world (در جهان)', with از marking the relation 'of/from' (i.e., 'one who remains as a trace of wisdom').
segments.s386-g37: A string of epithets joined by و (and). In Persian such noun/adjective labels can be used vocatively without an explicit vocative particle; here they address someone as 'wise, investigator, and proud'. The three words are simple nominative labels linked in coordination.
segments.s386-g38: چرا is the question word 'why'. بسته را is the definite/particular object marked by را (here 'the bound one' = captive); را marks it as definite/object. برد here is the past verb 'took' (the provided senses list did not include the verbal sense, so it is not matched); باید نماز is a short predicative clause meaning 'it is necessary to pray' (باید = 'must/it is necessary', نماز = 'prayer'). The three parts together ask why the captive was taken and then state that one must pray.
segments.s386-g39: کسی را ('someone' with the object/topic marker را) is followed by a relative clause introduced by که ('who/that') which modifies it. بر دست و پای آهنست is a poetic contracted copular clause (آهنست = آهنی است) meaning 'is of iron upon his hands and feet' — literally 'who has iron on hand and foot.' The preposition بر governs دست و پای ('on hand and foot').
segments.s386-g40: نه is the negation 'not' introducing the contrast. مردم نژادست is a predicate phrase meaning 'is of the people/race' (نژادست = 'is of lineage/kind'); the sense here is a denial that the person belongs to ordinary human stock. کهرمنست is a second predicate meaning 'is of amber' (a literary/metaphorical description). The structure is contrastive: 'not of human stock; (rather) of amber.'
segments.s386-g81: This clause is passive/affected-state: به + agent marks the captor ('by the Turks'), and اسیراند is a third-person plural verbal form meaning 'they are (held) captive' or 'they have been made captives'. با introduces accompaniment/condition ('with') and links the two nouns درد و داغ as co‑occurring afflictions describing their state.
segments.s386-g82: A compact participial/nominal phrase describing manner: پیاده 'on foot' sets manner, دوان is a present/participial form 'running, rushing', then the noun phrase رنگ رخ 'the color/complexion of the face' is compared with چون چراغ 'like a lamp' (چون = 'like'). Word order places manner and action before the descriptive simile.
segments.s386-g83: چنین ('thus') modifies the following noun پاسخ ('answer/reply'). آوردش is a past tense verb 'brought it' with the enclitic object -ش referring to 'him' (i.e., 'gave him' or 'replied to him'); the proper name اسفندیار appears after the verb (verb‑initial ordering is common in classical lines).
segments.s386-g84: That‑clause introduced by که reports Esfandiyar's content: من is the subject and بسته بودم is a past/stateive construction 'I was bound' (بسته = 'bound/tied/held' with بودم as the past auxiliary). چنین links to the preceding 'thus', and the coordinated adjectives زار و خوار describe his condition 'wretched and despised'.
segments.s386-g85: نکردند is the negative past plural 'they did not do/make'; زیشان 'by/from them' marks the source/agentive scope, ز + من 'of me' indicates the object of remembrance, هیچ intensifies the negation 'at all', and یاد is the noun 'remembrance/mention'. The clause therefore says they did not remember or make mention of me.
segments.s386-g86: نه is another negative particle; برزد is an archaic verb here meaning 'stirred/moved' or 'was affected' (third person). کس 'anyone' is negated by نه, and از بهر من is a prepositional phrase 'for my sake/on my behalf'. سردباد 'cold wind' stands at the end, used either literally or as a bleak image; the clause means 'no one was moved for me, (only) the cold wind.'
segments.s386-g87: چه is an interrogative 'what' and گویی here functions as 'words/speech' (a verbal noun usage related to گفتن) giving the sense 'what do you say'. به پاسخ 'as/for an answer' marks the target. که begins the content clause that follows (introducing what is being answered).
segments.s386-g88: ز من کرد یاد is a light verb construction: ز + من 'of me', کرد یاد 'made remembrance' = 'remembered me'. اندرین is a compound of اندر + این 'in this', and تنگ جای is a noun phrase 'narrow place' (i.e., 'in this cramped/narrow spot'). The whole clause reports the supposed single occasion of remembrance.
segments.s386-g89: دگر نیز (fronted modifier) = 'another / also' — دگر means 'another' here and نیز adds 'also'. پرمایه is the noun/adjective 'substantial, well-endowed' modifying the thing created. به آفرید is the verb phrase 'he created' with به as a classical prepositional/verbal particle attached to آفرید ('created'). Word order is poetic (modifier before noun before verb).
segments.s386-g90: که introduces a subordinate (reported) clause 'that / for' or 'because / which' here giving the content of what was said. گفتی is the past (perfect) 2nd‑person form 'you said' (the lemma senses supplied do not list this verbal sense, so treated below). م + را together mark the object 'm(e)' (here split in the annotation); در جهان خود is the locative phrase 'in your world' (خود = 'your own'). ندید is the negative past 'did not see'. The clause as a whole reports: 'that you said you did not see me in your own world.'
segments.s386-g91: بدو = 'to him' (prepositional clitic meaning 'unto him'); گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (the lemma senses given are for the noun/idea of speech, so the verbal sense is indicated in the explanation); جاماسپ is the speaker's name (Jamasp). کای پهلوان is a vocative: کای = 'O' and پهلوان = 'champion, hero'. The order is inverted (to him said Jamasp).
segments.s386-g92: پدرت is the noun پدر 'father' plus the 2nd‑person possessive suffix ت, 'your father.' از جهان تیره is a prepositional phrase 'from the dark/bleak world' (تیره = 'dark/ominous' here). دارد روان uses دارد as an auxiliary sense 'is (in the process of) / is going' and روان = 'soul, spirit' — together meaning 'his spirit is departing' or 'is leaving the world.'
segments.s386-g93: به کوه اندرست = 'is within the mountain' — به + کوه = 'into/to the mountain' and اندرست is an archaic copular/locative verb 'is within.' این زمان = 'at this time' (temporal adjunct). با سران = 'with the chiefs/heads' (با + plural سران). Word order is poetic: locative + copula + time phrase + accompaniment.
segments.s386-g94: دو دیده پر از آب = 'both eyes full of water' where دو modifies دیده ('two/both eyes'), پر (here postpositive) = 'full (of)', and از آب = 'of water' (i.e. tears). و لب ناچران = 'and the lip compelled/reluctant' — لب = 'lip' and ناچران describes its state ('unable/unwilling', i.e. constrained). Adjectival phrases follow nouns in this poetic register.
segments.s386-g95: سپاهی ز ترکان = 'a force/host from the Turks' (سپاهی = army/host; ز = poetic 'from'). بگرد here functions as an intransitive/poetic verb meaning 'is round/about' or 'surrounds' (not the imperative); the supplied dictionary senses were imperative/command, so that verbal sense is flagged below. اندر + ش = 'in/around him' with the enclitic ش = 'his/him.' The whole phrase means 'a Turkish army surrounds him.'
segments.s386-g96: همانا = emphatic 'indeed/verily.' نبینی here is negative present/gnomic or prophetic second person 'you will not (or do not) see' (in classical Persian this form is used for admonition or prediction). سر و افسرش = 'his head and his helmet' (سر = head; افسر = helmet) coordinated by و; ش on افسرش is the possessive clitic 'his.'
segments.s386-g121: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. آواز is the noun 'voice/call' and دادش is the verb داد + enclitic ش ('gave to him') used idiomatically with آواز دادن = 'to call'; here the construction means 'a voice called to him' or 'he was called'. ز marks the source/agent 'from/by', and فرشیدورد is the proper name causing the call.
segments.s386-g122: دلش is دل + enclitic ش = 'his heart'. گشت is the past of گشتن used copularly 'became'. پرخون is an adjectival compound 'full/besmeared with blood'. The conjunction و links the second nominal predicate; جان پر ز درد is an elided clause meaning '(his) life full of pain' where پر = 'full (of)' and ز = 'of'.
segments.s386-g123: چو = 'when'. باز آمدش دل is an inverted order ('again/returned came his heart') meaning his composure/heart returned. به جاماسپ گفت = 'he said to Jamasp' (به marks the indirect object/recipient). گفت here introduces the reported speech that follows.
segments.s386-g124: که is the complementizer introducing the content clause ('that' / here introducing a direct question). این بد = 'this evil' (این = this, بد = bad/evil). چرا = 'why'. داشتی is the 2nd-person past imperfect form of داشتن used here as 'you had/kept' (past). در نهفت = 'hidden inside' (در = in/inside, نهفت = hidden/concealed).
segments.s386-g125: بفرمای is an imperative/reporting verb (from فرمود/فرما) used to render an order or command ('he ordered/say:'). کاهنگران is the plural noun 'priests'. آورند is the 3rd-person plural present/historic form 'they bring/they brought' used here to report the effect of the order.
segments.s386-g126: چو = 'when'. سوهان و پتک گران آورند is a clause 'they brought files and a heavy sledgehammer' in which گران ('heavy') modifies پتک (and by position can be taken to qualify the preceding nouns), and آورند is the verb repeated for the action.
segments.s386-g127: بیاورد is the past/historic 3rd-person form 'he brought'. In classical word order the verb may come before subject, so بیاورد جاماسپ آهنگران reads 'Jamasp brought the blacksmiths' (جاماسپ = subject, آهنگران = object).
segments.s386-g128: چو here introduces accompanying instruments/items ('with' / 'along with' or 'namely') — سندان پولاد = 'an anvil of steel' (سندان = anvil, پولاد = steel), و پتک گران = 'and a heavy hammer' where گران = 'heavy' qualifies پتک.
segments.s386-g129: بسودند is a past indicative verb (they used/put into service); here it introduces what they employed. The following nouns are a series of direct objects (زنجیر، مسمار، غل) linked by و 'and' — a simple list of restraints they applied.
segments.s386-g130: همان بند رومی is a noun phrase 'that same Roman bond/fetter' (همان = 'that/same' before بند = 'fetter, bond'). به کردار combines به + کردار to mean 'in the manner/form (of)' and پل is the noun 'bridge' — together 'in the form of a bridge.'
segments.s386-g131: چو شد introduces a temporal clause 'when it became...' (چو = 'when', شد = 'became'). دیر بر سودن is an idiomatic phrase 'too late to avail / late for being useful' (دیر = 'late', بر + سودن = 'to avail/be of use'). بستگی is the noun 'the fastening' that is the topic of the main clause.
segments.s386-g132: This clause describes how the fastening was made: به بد تنگدل is an agent/attribute phrase 'by a bad/cruel, narrow‑hearted (man)' (به here marks the agent/means), بسته is a participial adjective 'bound, tied', and از خستگی gives the reason 'out of fatigue' (از = 'because of', خستگی = 'fatigue').
segments.s386-g133: به آهنگران گفت is 'he said to the blacksmiths' (به marks the indirect object). کای شوربخت is a vocative address 'O wretched/ill‑fated one' (کای = vocative). The verb گفت here introduces direct speech.
segments.s386-g134: ببندی is a 2nd person present/subjunctive form 'you bind' (imperative-like address). و simply links clauses. بسته is a participle/adjective 'when/once bound'; ندانی is 'you do not know' (negative 2nd person); گسخت is a past participle/adjective 'has come undone/broken' describing the state the speaker says they fail to notice.
segments.s386-g135: همی گفت is a habitual/progressive marker + verb 'he kept saying' (همی adds continuous sense). من بند آن شهریار is the object phrase 'I (the maker of) that king's fetter' or more literally 'I [regard] the bond of that king' — here identifying the speaker and the fetter under discussion.
segments.s386-g136: نکردم is the negative past 'I did not make/do'; به پیش is a prepositional phrase 'in the presence of / before' (به + پیش functioning together); خردمند خوار is a noun phrase 'the wise [who] despise' or more naturally 'the wise who would despise it' — overall: 'I did not make it to be despised before the wise.'
segments.s386-g97: نیاید here is a negative-present impersonal/predicative form meaning ‘is not pleasing’ or ‘does not please’; پسند is the noun ‘approval/liking’ and follows the verb in this poetic order; جهان‌آفرین is the agent in genitival/appositive position (‘the world‑creator’). Together the three form ‘(it) is not pleasing to the Creator of the world’.
segments.s386-g98: که introduces the subordinate clause ‘that…’; تو is the 2nd‑person pronoun subject; دل is the direct object ‘heart’; بپیچی is the present/subjunctive/optative 2nd‑person singular of پیچیدن used here as ‘you turn’ or ‘you twist’; the preposition ز (poetic form of از) marks separation ‘from’, repeated before مهر ‘love’ and دین ‘religion’. The clause thus reads ‘that you turn your heart from love and from religion.’
segments.s386-g99: برادر is a vocative address here (‘brother’). که begins a relative/explanatory clause. بد is an adjective ‘bad/ill’ (poetic usage); مر is the clitic meaning ‘me/to me’ and ت is the 2nd‑person enclitic attached to the preceding element (common in older Persian word order); را is the object/definite marker. The clause is elliptical/poetic and lists a number (سی و هشت) associated with the speaker and addressee: ‘Brother, (it was) bad to me/for me — thirty‑eight.’
segments.s386-g100: ازان ‘of those / from them’ is followed by the past form ماند (‘remained/was left’) with the numeral پنج as the notional subject — ‘five remained’. و links to دگر درگذشت, where دگر ‘the others/the rest’ is the subject and درگذشت is past tense ‘passed away/died’. The clause therefore contrasts the five survivors with the rest who died.
segments.s386-g101: چنین + پاسخ + آوردش is a fixed poetic arrangement: چنین (‘thus’) + پاسخ (‘reply/answer’) + آوردش (آورد + ـش = ‘brought it to him’ / ‘gave him’). اسفندیار is the subject. The verb آوردش is past tense with the clitic object ـش (‘him’), so the phrase means ‘Esfandiyar thus gave/returned the reply to him’ or more idiomatically ‘Esfandiyar answered him thus.’
segments.s386-g102: که begins the reported content clause. چندین is an indefinite numeral ‘several/many a’; برادر is the noun ‘brothers’. بدم here is an older/poetic form meaning ‘of my blood / of my kin’ (i.e. ‘my’ or ‘of my lineage’); نامدار is an adjective ‘renowned/illustrious’. The sequence gives the content: ‘that (I) had several brothers, illustrious kin.’
segments.s386-g103: همه شاد با رامش is a verbless nominal clause meaning ‘all [were] glad/joyful with peace’ (poetic omission of کردن/بودن). و connects to من به بند, where من is the pronoun ‘I’ and به بند literally ‘in bonds’ (به + بند; بند = fetter/bond) indicating captivity or constraint. The contrast is drawn between the others’ happiness and the speaker’s bondage.
segments.s386-g104: نکردند is the past negative plural ‘they did not do’; یاد functions as an object meaning ‘remembrance’ so together ‘they did not remember’. از marks the object’s relation (‘of/about’), followed by من ‘me’; مستمند is a predicate/appositive ‘the needy/poor’ describing the speaker. The whole clause reads ‘they did not remember me, (I who am) needy.’
segments.s386-g153: یکی functions as an indefinite subject 'a certain / one (of them)' placed before the noun. جوشن خسروانی is a noun + adjectival postmodifier (جوشن 'cuirass/armor' + خسروانی 'royal'); بخواست is the simple past (classical Persian b‑prefix) meaning 'asked for' or 'desired' and agrees with the implied third‑person subject.
segments.s386-g154: همان is a demonstrative/emphatic 'that/the same'; جامه پهلوانی is a noun phrase where پهلوانی (literally 'heroism') works adjectivally to mean 'heroic / of a hero'. The verb بخواست (past) repeats the action; the clause thus restates the request with emphasis.
segments.s386-g155: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past 'he commanded/said'. کان باره is an adverbial phrase ('on/at that occasion' or 'then') formed from کان + باره (باره = 'instance/occasion'). گام زن is a compact descriptive expression ('one who steps' / 'the stepper' or 'the marcher') formed from گام 'step' + زن (a verbal/doer element); the clause ends with the imperative implied by the following verb.
segments.s386-g156: بیارید is the polite/imparative plural 'bring (here)' (classical form with b‑). The coordinating و links items; آن ترگ و شمشیر من is a noun phrase 'that rag and my sword' with من after شمشیر as a possessive 'my'; the sequence is a list of items commanded to be brought.
segments.s386-g157: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. چشمش is the noun 'his eye / his gaze' with the enclitic -ش for 'his'. بران is an adverb/prepositional particle 'on/upon that' referring back to something just mentioned. تیزرو is a compound adjective 'swift‑running' (تیز 'sharp/fast' + رو 'going/run‑suffix'). برفتاد is past tense with the prefix بر‑ + فتاد, here 'turned/moved (toward)' — together meaning 'his eye turned to / fell upon'.
segments.s386-g158: ز is the poetic genitive/from preposition 'from/of'. یزدان is the divine agent 'God'. نیکی is the abstract noun 'goodness' or 'good deed'. دهش is a noun 'gift/grant' (here part of the compound idea 'the giving/manifestation of goodness'). کرد is the past auxiliary 'did/made'. یاد is a noun 'remembrance' — the whole clause literally 'he made remembrance of God's goodness', i.e. 'he remembered/credited God's goodness'.
segments.s386-g159: همی is a continuative adverb 'kept / still' indicating repeated or habitual speech. گفت‌گر is an archaic/formal marker introducing reported speech or a conditional sense ('as he would say' / 'he kept saying'); here it frames the following quoted thought. من گنه کرده‌ام is the first‑person present‑perfect 'I have sinned' (گنه 'sin' + کرده‌ام 'I have done').
segments.s386-g160: ازینسان is an adverb 'thus / in this way'. به بند اندر is a prepositional phrase 'into/within bond(s)' (به + بند + اندر = 'in/into bondage'). آزرده‌ام is a stative perfect 'I am distressed/hurt' (آزرده with the first‑person suffix ‑ام). Together the clause expresses the speaker's lament 'I am thus bound and distressed.'
segments.s386-g57: دلت‌گر is the conditional phrase 'دلت گر' = 'if your heart' (گر attaches to the possessor). چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' modifying the state. از پدر is a prepositional phrase 'from/about the father' indicating cause/target of the astonishment. خیره گشت is a two-word predicate: خیره 'astonished/stunned' (adjectival/predicative) + گشت (past of گشت, 'became') — together 'became astonished'. Word order places the conditional subject first and the verbal predicate at the end, as is common in classical Persian.
segments.s386-g58: نگر is an imperative/interjection 'look!/behold!'. بخت is the noun 'fortune/fate' and is followed by the genitive-like phrase این پادشا 'this king' (Persian often places the determiner/possessive after the noun: 'the fortune of this king'). تیره گشت is the predicate 'turned dark/ominous' where تیره is the adjective 'dark/ominous' and گشت is the past 'became'.
segments.s386-g59: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. لهراسپ شاه = 'Lohrasp the king' (name + title). آن پرستنده مرد is an appositive phrase 'that devout/ worshipping man' (آن = 'that', پرستنده modifies مرد). The whole segment sets up a temporal-relative frame 'When Lohrasp…'.
segments.s386-g60: که is the relative pronoun 'who/whom' linking to 'مرد'. ترکان is the subject 'the Turks'. بکشتندش is the past verb 'they killed him' (ب- prefix + کشتند, with the clitic '-ش' = 'him'). اندر نبرد is a locative/time phrase 'in battle' (اندر = 'in/among', نبرد = 'battle'). The clause is a relative clause describing the preceding man.
segments.s386-g61: همان is the demonstrative 'the same/that very'. هیربد is the title 'hirbad' (chief priest/magus). نیز 'also/too' adds likeness. یزدان‌پرست is a descriptive noun/adjective 'God‑worshipper' modifying هیربد. Word order places the determiner and title first, with the descriptive term after.
segments.s386-g62: که is the relative pronoun 'who'. بودند is the past plural of 'to be' ('they were' / 'he/they had' in older Persian usage — here a stative past). با زند و استا به دست is a prepositional phrase: با 'with', زند 'Zand' (the exegetical text), و 'and', استا (here intended as اوستا/Avesta — the sacred book; the given lexical sense list lacks that meaning), به دست 'in (his) hand' meaning 'holding'. Overall: 'who were holding the Zand and the Avesta in their hands.'
segments.s386-g63: بکشتند is the past verb 'they killed' (note the older prefix ب-). هشتاد is the numeral 'eighty' as the object; Persian poetry permits the verb to precede its object. از موبدان is a partitive 'of/from among the mobeds' (موبدان = mobeds, Zoroastrian priests).
segments.s386-g64: پرستنده و پاک‌دل بخردان are appositive/descriptive nouns modifying the killed mobeds: پرستنده 'worshipping/devout', و 'and', پاک‌دل 'pure‑hearted', and بخردان (plural of 'wise/prudent one') 'wise men/sages'. The phrase lists the priests' qualities.
segments.s386-g1: This is a nominal phrase in the order determiner/adjective + noun + patronymic: 'یکی' (a certain) + 'مایه‌ور' (wealthy) qualifies 'پور اسفندیار' (son/descendant of Esfandiyar). 'پور' is the classical element meaning 'son of' and follows the adjective that modifies the whole noun phrase.
segments.s386-g2: 'که' introduces a relative clause (‘whom/that’). 'نوش آذرش' is here a personal name (the compound Nush-Azar) with the enclitic -ش marking the object/possessive; the verb 'خواندی' is an older past form meaning 'you (or one) called/named' and takes 'نوش آذرش' as its object; 'شهریار' is an apposition meaning 'the king'. Note the classical/poetic ordering where the relative clause follows the noun it qualifies.
segments.s386-g3: 'بران' is an adverbial/prepositional particle meaning 'thereon/thence'; 'بام دژ' is a genitive/attributive sequence 'the roof (summit) of the fortress'; 'بود' is the past copula 'was' (here narrative past); 'و' links two coordinated predicates; 'چشمش به راه' is an idiomatic verbless clause meaning 'his eye was on the road' (i.e. he kept watch). The order is typical classical Persian: locative/adverbial + noun phrase + copula, then coordination.
segments.s386-g4: This clause begins with the fused verbal idea 'بدان' (here be+دان = 'to know/see/observe'), followed by the subordinating 'تا کی' = 'until when' and the verb 'آید' (classical present used for future/habitual: 'will come'). 'ز ایران سپاه' is a prepositional phrase 'a host from Iran' that functions as the subject/agent of 'آید' in poetic word order. The whole phrase expresses purpose/intent: 'to see until when a host comes from Iran.'
segments.s386-g5: 'پدر را' is marked with the object/topic marker 'را' and functions as the indirect object 'to his father' (classical Persian often uses را for definite objects or topics). 'بگوید' is a classical present/subjunctive-form verb used in narrative (here 'he tells' or 'he would tell'); 'چو' introduces a temporal clause 'when/whenever'; 'بیند کسی' is 'he sees someone/anyone' (verb before subject 'کسی' is normal in classical word order).
segments.s386-g6: 'به بالای دژ' is a prepositional phrase 'to/on the summit of the fortress'; 'درنمانده' is a perfect/passive‑looking negative participle meaning 'have not remained / were not left' (classical compound negation in past meaning); 'بسی' is a classical adverb of quantity/emphasis ('many/very' in context). The verb phrase follows the locative phrase, consistent with Persian verb-final tendencies in classical diction.
segments.s386-g7: 'چو' = 'when' introduces the temporal clause. 'جاماسپ را دید پویان به راه' follows classical Persian word order where the object-marked name 'جاماسپ را' comes before the verb 'دید' (saw), then the actor 'پویان' (here a proper name) and the locative 'به راه' (on the road). This yields 'When Puyan saw Jamasp on the road...' with subject/object positions determined by the object marker 'را'.
segments.s386-g8: 'به سربر' = 'on (to) the head' (sarbَر), a locative phrase; 'یکی نغز' is an adjectival phrase 'a certain fine/beautiful' (one + 'naghz' = 'a handsome/dainty'); 'توزی' is an archaic/obscure verbal/derivational element here attaching to the noun phrase (its exact lexical meaning is archaic and uncertain in the gloss); 'کلاه' is the noun 'hat/cap'. The order reflects classical Persian stacking of locative + quantifier/adjective + (verb/obscure element) + head noun: 'he placed / set a handsome cap on his head.'
segments.s386-g49: This is a poetic word order: the past participle 'بسته' (bound/tied) comes before the modal 'باید' to form 'must be bound'. 'کنون همچنین' are fronted adverbs ('now, likewise'). 'تنم' is the possessed noun 'my body' (possessive -م) and is the thing that must be bound.
segments.s386-g50: 'به یزدان' is a prepositional phrase 'before/to God'. 'گوای' is a noun meaning 'witness' and 'منست' is an adjectival/predicative word 'is fitting/appropriate'; together they say that 'آهنم' (my iron/weapon) stands as a witness before God — 'آهنم' is the item being predicated.
segments.s386-g51: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause 'that...'. 'بر من' = 'against me / to me' (showing victim/target). 'ز' marks the agent/source 'by/from Gushtasp'. 'بیداد بود' is a nominal predicate 'there was injustice' (بود = was).
segments.s386-g52: 'ز' here gives the cause/source: 'from/by the mention'. 'گفت' is used as a noun meaning 'speech/mention' (the wording/mention of something). 'گرزم' is the weapon 'my mace' (the thing mentioned). The clause ends with 'اهرمن شاد بود' = 'Ahriman was glad' (شاد بود = rejoiced/was happy).
segments.s386-g53: 'مبادا که' is a negative optative/concessive phrase meaning 'may it not be that / lest'. 'فرامش کنم' is a present-subjunctive construction ('that I should forget'); the object of this wish is 'این بد' = 'this evil/ill' (the thing he fears forgetting).
segments.s386-g54: 'روان را' marks 'the soul/spirit' as the definite object (را). 'به گفتار بیهش کنم' is a causative/result clause: 'to/into (his) speech/words (بیهش) I should make (کنم)' — i.e. 'subject my soul to his words.' 'کنم' again is subjunctive/capable-of-happening mood linked to 'مبادا'.
segments.s386-g55: 'بدو' = 'to him' (prepositional pronoun). The verb 'گفت' = 'said' is followed by the speaker's name 'جاماسپ' (Jamasp) — poetic/inverted order. 'کای' is a vocative 'O' (addressing a king/title), and 'راست‌گوی' is an epithet 'truthful' addressing the addressee.
segments.s386-g56: This segment lists epithets/names joined by و ('and'): 'جهانگیر' (world-conquering/conqueror), 'کنداور' (another epithet/name), and 'نیک‌خوی' (good-natured/virtuous). In Persian the conjunction simply strings attributes or names together.
segments.s386-g25: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when' (classical/poetic usage). بشنید is a past tense verb 'he heard' placed before its object (typical Persian order here). نوش آذر is a personal name (compound); از + پهلوان means 'from/of the champion' – از marks the source or report.
segments.s386-g26: بیامد is a past tense verb 'he came'. بران باره is a fixed phrase 'on that occasion' or 'thereupon' (بران = 'on/thereon', باره = 'occasion'), and دژ دوان strings noun + participle/verb: دژ 'fortress' followed by دوان 'rushed/run', indicating motion toward the fortress.
segments.s386-g27: چو marks a temporal 'when' clause. جاماسپ is the proper name (subject). تنگ اندر آمد ز راه is a compact expression: تنگ 'close/closely' + اندر آمد 'came in/entered' + ز راه 'from the road', so the adverbial 'closely' precedes the verb as in classical style.
segments.s386-g28: هم at the start adds 'also' or 'too'. از باره 'of the matter/affair' uses از to mark 'concerning' and باره 'the affair/occasion'; دانست is past tense 'he knew/learned' and is followed by its object فرزند شاه 'the king's son' (noun + genitive).
segments.s386-g29: بیامد is past 'he came'. به نزدیک 'to near' is a locative phrase indicating approach; the adjective/epithet فرخ qualifies پدر ('Farrokh/the fortunate father') and follows the usual modifier–noun order here (epithet then noun).
segments.s386-g30: که here introduces a subordinate clause meaning 'for/because/that': 'for the auspicious Jamasp came to the gate/court.' فرخنده is an adjective 'auspicious/fortunate' modifying جاماسپ; آمد is simple past 'came' and به در uses در 'door/gate' to mean 'to the court/presence'.
segments.s386-g31: بفرمود is a courteous/polite past form 'he commanded/said'. تا here functions as a subordinating 'so that'/'that' (introducing the command's content). دژ is the object 'the fortress'; گشادند باز is a verbal phrase meaning 'were opened wide' (گشادند shows plural/agentive past action and باز indicates 'open/wide').
segments.s386-g32: درآمد here functions as 'entered' (verb of coming in). خردمند 'the wise man' is the agent; و links clauses. بردش is a contracted form meaning 'he took/carried him' (برد + ـش = 'him'), and نماز is the destination or purpose 'prayer' (here likely 'to the place of prayer' or 'to perform/offer prayer').
segments.s386-g113: چه starts the question/interrogative 'what'. گویی here is the archaic/present form 'you say' (second-person), with کنون 'now' as a temporal adverb placed after the verb; کارِ فرشیدورد is the object phrase 'the affair/case of Farshidvard' (کار + name), placed after the verb phrase as is common in older Persian.
segments.s386-g114: که introduces a relative clause modifying 'کار' ("which/that"). بود is the past copula 'was'. از تو here means 'because of you' / 'on your part' (source or causal sense). همواره 'always' qualifies the clause, and با داغ و درد 'with burning grief and pain' gives the manner/contents; the whole clause describes the affair as repeatedly accompanied by sorrow.
segments.s386-g115: به هر سو که بودی is a conditional/relative phrase 'to/at whatever side you were' (که introducing the clause, بودی = 'you were', past). The pairs به رزم و به بزم use به with nouns to mean 'in battle and in the banquet' (contrasting locales/occasions).
segments.s386-g116: پر از + noun means 'full of ...' (پر = 'full', از = 'of'). درد و نفرین = 'pain and curse(s)'. بدی here functions as the noun 'evil/harm' modifying or intensifying 'نفرین' ('an evil curse' or 'harmful curse'); بر گرزم ('upon my mace') marks the target with بر + گرزم (گرز + possessive -م 'my'). The exact syntactic relation is poetic/elliptical, but the sense is that he brought pain and hurled evil curses upon the speaker's mace.
segments.s386-g117: پر از زخمِ شمشیر = 'full of sword-wounds' (پر از + noun phrase). دیدم is first-person past 'I saw' and takes تنش ('his body' = تن + enclitic ش) as its object; the typical classical Persian order places the descriptive prepositional phrase before the verb and the possessed noun after it.
segments.s386-g118: دریده = 'torn' (adjective/past participle). برو here is a compressed/poetic sequence (literally 'بر او' = 'upon him') used with the following nouns; مغفر و جوشنش list two garments/armors (مغفر 'covering/veil', جوشن 'cuirass') and جوشن has the possessive suffix -ش ('his'). So the phrase says his cloak and armor were torn upon him.
segments.s386-g119: همی is an iterative/adverbial particle 'continually, still'. زار here is adverbial/participial meaning 'in lament/lamely' or 'wailing'. می‌بگسلد is present/habitual 3rd-person 'loosens/slips away' (mi- prefix = continuous aspect). جان اوی = 'his life' (جان + old possessive اوی). The clause describes his life repeatedly slipping away amid lamentation.
segments.s386-g120: ببخشای is an exhortation/entreaty 'forgive' or 'grant mercy' (archaic/poetic imperative/optative). بر چشم گریان اوی = 'upon his weeping eyes' (بر marking the beneficiary/target or reason, چشم گریان = 'weeping eye(s)', اوی = 'his'). The speaker petitions for mercy because of his tears.
segments.s386-g65: ز خونشان is a prepositional/circumstantial phrase 'from their blood' (ز + possessive شان). به نوش‌آذر آذر is the personal name being referenced (the preposition به here introduces or attaches to the name in poetic diction). بمرد is an archaic narrative past/perfect 3rd‑person form (the b‑ prefix appears in classical Persian pasts) meaning 'died'. The whole segment therefore states the cause and the event: 'Nush‑Azar Azar died from their blood.'
segments.s386-g66: چنین modifies the following noun (it = 'such'). بدکنش is a compound noun 'ill‑doer' (literally 'bad‑doer'). خوار is a predicative adjective 'despised/despicable'. نتوان + شمرد is a fixed negative modal construction: نتوان (cannot) + bare verb شمرد ('to count/consider') gives 'cannot be counted/considered'. Together: 'One cannot consider such an ill‑doer despicable.'
segments.s386-g67: ز بهر (for the sake of) introduces the purpose or motive; نیا here is 'ancestor' (object of بهر). دل پر از درد کن is an imperative clause: دل 'heart' + پر از 'full of' + درد 'pain/sorrow' + کن (imperative 'make/do'), so 'make the heart full of sorrow.'
segments.s386-g68: This is a second imperative coordinated to the first. برآشوب is an imperative/causative sense 'raise up a tumult/disturb'; و is the conjunction 'and'; رخسارگان (plural) 'the faces/countenances'; زرد is adjective 'pale/wan'; کن is the same imperative 'make'. Grammatically, two commands are linked: 'Raise a tumult and make the faces pale.'
segments.s386-g69: ز کین یا ز دین‌گر 'out of hatred/vengeance or out of the pious/religious zeal' sets two motives. نجنبی is a negative instruction formed from the root جنبیدن (here serving as 'do not be moved / do not be roused'); ز جای 'from your place' indicates the source/location. Together this yields a prohibitory clause: 'Do not be moved from your place out of vengeance or pious fervor.'
segments.s386-g70: نباشی is a form of باشیدن/بودن used here to express the (bad) result or state — 'you will not be / you must not be' — and is followed by the predicative phrase پسندیده رهنمای: پسندیده 'approved/acceptable' modifying رهنمای 'guide'. The whole line warns that if one is moved thus, 'you will not be an approved guide.'
segments.s386-g71: چنین داد پاسخ is a narrative clause: چنین 'thus' + داد 'gave' + پاسخ 'an answer' (i.e. 'he answered thus'). که‌ای introduces the reported speech; ای + نیک‌نام is a vocative 'O well‑named / O famous one' addressing someone directly.
segments.s386-g72: This segment lists epithets applied to the addressee. بلنداختر is a compound epithet 'high‑star(ed)' (an honorific), و is 'and', گرد here participates in the string of epithets, و again 'and', جوینده is the active participle 'seeker', and کام is its object 'desire' or 'wish' — 'seeker of desire'. Grammatically these are nominal epithets coordinated by و following the vocative.
segments.s386-g137: بپیچید is a past, 3rd-person singular verbal form of پیچیدن (here 'he twisted/turned'); تن را is the definite direct object (را marks 'the body'); و links the two actions; بر پای جست places the directional particle بر + پای ('onto/to the feet') before the past verb جست ('he leapt/sprang'), so the clause reads 'he sprang to his feet.'
segments.s386-g138: غمی شد is an idiomatic past construction ('a cry/pain/anguish arose' or 'he uttered a cry') using شد as 'became/occurred'; the sequence یازید دست ... به پابند is an inverted word order: the normal pattern دست یازید به پابند ('he laid/strew his hand upon the fetter' = 'he reached for/seized the shackle') is here inverted to به پابند یازید دست, so یازید governs 'دست' and the phrase means his hand was thrust toward the fetter.
segments.s386-g139: بیاهیخت پای is a predicative phrase: بی‌آهیخت (literally 'disordered, thrown out of joint') describes the state of پای ('his foot'); the copula is omitted (classical Persian often omits بودن). و links the next action; بپیچید دست again places the verb before the noun (verb-initial order) meaning 'his hand twisted' or 'he twisted his hand.'
segments.s386-g140: همه quantifies the following nouns ('all/entirely'); بند و زنجیر is a coordinated noun phrase 'fetters and chains'; بر هم is an adverbial expression meaning 'together' or 'all at once'; شکَست is the past tense 'broke' (here effectively passive in sense: 'were broken' / 'broke apart together').
segments.s386-g141: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; بگسست is the simple past 'broke apart/snapped' with زنجیر as its object; بی‌توش گشت is a predicative clause using the prefix بی- 'without' plus توش (an archaic/poetic word here meaning strength/sustenance/support or sense), with گشت 'became' — so 'he became without strength/disoriented' on the breaking of the chain.
segments.s386-g142: بیفتاد is the past tense 'he fell'; از درد is a prepositional phrase giving cause ('from/out of pain' = 'because of the pain'); و coordinates the final result clause; بیهوش گشت uses بیهوش ('unconscious') plus گشت ('became') to state the result 'he became unconscious.'
segments.s386-g143: ستاره شمرکان is a genitive/attributive phrase 'the star of the Shamarakan' (an omen-star or star belonging to that people/party); شگفتی is the object 'a wonder/astonishment'; بدید is the past 'saw' — together 'the Shamarakan star saw a wonder' (i.e., an extraordinary omen occurred).
segments.s386-g144: بران = 'on/for that (event)/thereupon'; تاجدار is 'the crown-bearer' (the sovereign); آفرین here means 'acclamation/praise' (not the verb 'to create'); گسترید is past/pluralic poetic form 'spread forth/poured out' — together: 'thereupon the king showered forth praise.'
